symbols: serpent coiled in tree — Satan the deceiver, the ancient enemy of Genesis 3 and Revelation 12:9
forbidden fruit — disobedience to God's command and the desire for forbidden knowledge
fig leaves held by Eve — shame and the first human attempt to cover sin after the Fall
nakedness — original innocence now turning to vulnerability and exposure before God
Adam reaching upward toward serpent — active participation in the transgression, not passive ignorance
Eve's pale luminous flesh contrasted with Adam's darker skin — possible visual distinction of the two created beings, male and female
tree — the Tree of the Knowledge of Good and Evil, the locus of the divine prohibition
dark curtain or canopy — the shadow of death entering creation through sin
description: A tall, narrow composition showing two nude figures standing beside a tree. Adam, seen from behind with muscular, tanned skin, reaches upward toward a serpent coiled among the branches above. Eve stands facing the viewer, her pale body fully visible, holding a small sprig of fig leaves at her waist. The serpent twists around the upper trunk and branches of the tree. The background is dark, with a deep blue-green drapery or canopy behind the figures. A stone or rock is visible at the base near their feet.
theological_description: This image captures the decisive moment of the Fall, with Adam actively reaching toward the serpent and the forbidden fruit while Eve already holds fig leaves — suggesting the transgression is in progress or just completed. The scene condenses the entire drama of Genesis 3 into a single frame. The Fathers of the Church, particularly Irenaeus and Augustine, saw in this moment the catastrophic reversal of the divine order: the creature choosing autonomy over obedience, knowledge of evil over trust in God. Adam's active reach toward the serpent counters the common exegetical tradition that blamed Eve alone; here both are complicit. Eve's fig leaves evoke Genesis 3:7, the first act after the eyes of both were opened — the pitiful human attempt to cover guilt that only God's own provision of animal skins (Gen 3:21) would properly address, a foreshadowing of sacrificial atonement. Typologically, the tree of disobedience finds its reversal in the wood of the Cross, where the Second Adam undoes the ruin of the first (Rom 5:12-21). The serpent here prefigures the one whom Christ will crush (Gen 3:15, the Protoevangelium), and the nakedness of shame will be restored to glory only in the Resurrection body.

scripture_refs.reason: The central event depicted: Eve takes the fruit and gives also to Adam, who eats
The fig leaves Eve holds signal the moment their eyes were opened and they knew they were naked
The serpent's temptation in the tree, visible coiled above the figures
The Protoevangelium — God's promise that the seed of the woman will crush the serpent's head, the first messianic prophecy
Through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin — the Pauline theology of the Fall as universal catastrophe
By one man's disobedience the many were made sinners; the Adam-Christ typology is implicit in any depiction of the Fall
In Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive — the Fall demands the Resurrection
The ancient serpent identified as the devil and Satan, connecting Eden's tempter to the cosmic adversary
